questions.question: Why is separating hot and cold data useful in system design?
options: Because all data naturally becomes cold at the same rate.
Because a small working set often drives most latency-sensitive traffic and deserves a different cost profile.
Because cold data never needs backups or integrity checks.
Because tiering removes the need for indexes.
questions.explanation: Hot and cold data have different performance and cost requirements, so treating them identically is wasteful.

questions.question: What is a hot row or hot key?
options: A row that is encrypted with a newer key version.
A record that receives a disproportionate amount of read or write traffic.
A row that is stored only in memory.
A record that failed a checksum.
questions.explanation: Hotspots are about skewed traffic concentration, not storage medium alone.

questions.question: Which metric best exposes that one tenant is creating a dangerous storage hotspot?
options: Overall fleet average latency without tenant breakdown.
Read and write skew metrics by tenant or key class.
Average size of deployment artifacts.
Only total number of archived objects.
questions.explanation: Averages hide skew, while per-tenant or per-key metrics reveal concentration.

questions.question: What is the hidden risk of an archive tier that has never been restored in practice?
options: It will make the hot path faster than expected.
It may fail exactly when compliance, audit, or incident recovery depends on it.
It eliminates all metadata consistency issues.
It automatically reduces write hotspots.
questions.explanation: An untested restore path is an operational risk, not a real capability.

questions.question: When is caching alone not enough to solve hot/cold data problems?
options: When long-term storage cost and archive lifecycle requirements matter in addition to hot reads.
When the application uses HTTP instead of gRPC.
When the database already has a primary key.
When the working set is smaller than memory.
questions.explanation: Caching helps hot reads, but it does not replace lifecycle, retention, and archive economics.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with moving older data to a cheaper archive tier?
options: Recent writes become impossible to replicate.
Long-term cost drops, but retrieval becomes slower and operationally more complex.
Consistency guarantees always improve automatically.
Secondary indexes no longer need maintenance.
questions.explanation: Archive tiers save money by trading away immediate low-latency access.

questions.question: Which design move best handles a write-heavy hot counter?
options: Force all writes through one stronger machine.
Split, shard, aggregate, or otherwise distribute the hotspot instead of letting one row absorb all pressure.
Store the counter in colder storage tiers.
Remove observability to reduce contention.
questions.explanation: Hot write concentration needs distribution or aggregation, not just more hope.

questions.question: What should a strong interview answer include when proposing tiering?
options: Only the storage classes and approximate cost savings.
How data is classified, when it moves, and how restore or replay works.
A statement that cold data is not important to users.
A promise that archived data will never be queried again.
questions.explanation: Tiering is a lifecycle and restore design, not just a storage pricing choice.
